cycles.name: Bedrock
focuses: Build a bulletproof, spine-sparing strength foundation and anti-movement core that makes the lower back robust under load.
Establish an easy aerobic base — self-managed Zone-2 walk/run at a sub-140 heart rate.

paragraphs: This is the foundation everything else gets built on. Before we make you a hybrid athlete, we make your back unbreakable and your movement clean — that's the platform Forge, Crossover and Hybrid all stand on. Skip it and the whole arc cracks.
Your job this block: own the brace before you chase the weight. Every working set leaves 1–2 reps in the tank — we're building tissue and trust, not testing limits.
By week 5: load climbing on every main lift, carries noticeably heavier, side planks rock-solid both sides — and a back that takes training instead of fearing it.
outcomes: Three sessions a week banked with zero back flare-ups
Load up every week on Leg Press and Hip Thrust
Clean 30-second side plank, both sides equal
Carry weight up across the block — suitcase and farmer
Brace locked on every main lift — no low-back cheat
Easy-run base started: two sub-140 runs a week in the bank

cycles.name: Forge
focuses: Progressively overload the main spine-sparing patterns to build real strength and lean muscle on the foundation.
Extend the aerobic base toward continuous easy running — volume before intensity.

paragraphs: Once the base holds, we stop protecting and start loading. Forge is where the strength actually gets built — heavier, and with the free movements your back will have earned the right to load.
The brace you build now is the permission slip.
Lay the bedrock. Then we forge.

cycles.name: Crossover
focuses: Run heavier, lower-volume strength alongside the first structured run intensity, managing the interference between them.
Hold trunk durability and movement quality as total weekly training load climbs.

cycles.name: Hybrid
focuses: Integrate strength and running into genuine hybrid capacity — perform and stay durable under combined demand.
Lock back resilience as a permanent baseline and reassess to set the next training arc.

days.completionMessage: Legs loaded, trunk braced — that's the foundation of every run and every lift to come. Walk easy, hydrate, let the hips recover.

good: Step into a deep lunge, drop the back knee tall
Feel the hip and mid-back open as you rotate
bad: Don't rush — move slow and own each position

good: Long arm and opposite leg, body in one straight line
Feel the trunk lock to resist any twist
bad: Don't let the low back sag or the hips tip

good: Push through the mid-foot, knees track over the toes
Feel the quads and glutes drive the sled
bad: Don't let the low back round off the pad at the bottom

good: Hinge from the hips, lift to a straight line — no higher
Feel the glutes and hamstrings, not the low back
bad: Don't hyperextend or crank past neutral at the top

good: Lower to the upper chest, elbows at roughly 45 degrees
Feel the upper chest stretch, then drive
bad: Don't flare the elbows wide or bounce off the chest

good: Drive the elbows back and pause at the top
Feel the mid-back squeeze the shoulder blades
bad: Don't shrug or yank with the arms

good: Press the low back flat, extend opposite arm and leg
Feel the deep abs hold the ribs down
bad: Don't let the back arch off the floor

good: Stand tall and walk with one heavy dumbbell at your side
Feel the opposite obliques fight the lean
bad: Don't tip toward the weight or shrug it up

days.completionMessage: Pressed strong and built the back that protects your spine. Roll the shoulders out tonight and keep that sleep honest.

good: Slowly round then arch the whole spine
Feel each segment move one at a time
bad: Don't force the end range — stay smooth

good: Arms straight, pull the band to your chest
Feel the rear delts and mid-back switch on
bad: Don't shrug the shoulders to your ears

good: Press straight up with the back flat on the pad
Feel the shoulders drive without the low back arching
bad: Don't push the ribs forward to cheat the press

good: Pull the bar to the upper chest, chest proud
Feel the lats drive the elbows down
bad: Don't lean way back or use momentum

good: Drive the hips to a flat tabletop and squeeze at the top
Feel the glutes finish the lockout
bad: Don't arch the low back to fake extra height

good: Pull the rope to your forehead, elbows high
Feel the rear delts and upper back
bad: Don't let the elbows drop or the head jut forward

good: Raise to shoulder height, leading with the elbows
Feel the side delts do the work
bad: Don't swing or shrug to lift the dumbbells

good: Press the handle straight out, hold, then return
Feel the trunk resist the cable's pull to rotate
bad: Don't let your torso twist toward the stack

days.completionMessage: Full-body work and a heavy carry to finish — that's real-world strength and conditioning in one. Loose walk later, and you've earned the rest day.

good: Deep lunge, rotate the top arm to the ceiling
Feel the hip flexor and T-spine open
bad: Don't let the front knee cave inward

good: Drive the hips up and squeeze the glutes at the top
Feel the glutes switch on before you load them
bad: Don't push through the low back instead of the hips

good: Sit straight down, chest tall, heels elevated
Feel the quads load through full depth
bad: Don't round the low back at the bottom

good: Drive the hips to a flat tabletop and squeeze at the top
Feel the glutes finish the lockout
bad: Don't arch the low back to fake extra height

good: Drive the elbows back and pause at the top
Feel the mid-back squeeze the shoulder blades
bad: Don't shrug or yank with the arms

good: Lower to mid-chest, elbows at roughly 45 degrees
Feel the chest stretch, then press
bad: Don't flare the elbows or bounce the dumbbells

good: Stack the body in one straight line, hips high
Feel the side of the trunk hold the line
bad: Don't let the hips sag toward the floor

good: Stand tall and walk with a heavy dumbbell in each hand
Feel the whole trunk and grip stay braced
bad: Don't lean back or let the shoulders round forward

cards.title: Brace Off The Gym Floor
cards.body: The Pallof, dead bug, and suitcase carry aren't just session work — they're a rehearsal for the 23 hours a day you're not training. Picking your bag off the floor, loading the car, standing up from a low chair: hinge at the hips, brace the trunk like you do under the Hip Thrust bar, and keep the load close. Whether your back stays manageable instead of flaring comes down to carrying that pattern into ordinary life, not just nailing three gym sessions. At 90 kg, bending badly over a sink is a bigger axial dose than most of what's in this program — so own the brace everywhere.

cards.title: Fix The 6/10, Not The 7 Hours
cards.body: You're banking 7–8 hours but only rating it 6/10 — non-restorative sleep is the single biggest brake on this cycle, because moderate recovery is what sets your RPE-8 cap and your conservative volume. Quantity is fine; quality is the lever, so attack the inputs: screens off 60 minutes before bed, room cold and black, no caffeine after midday. Your Day 1 and Day 3 sessions both finish on heavy carries, and your nervous system clears that load overnight — protect the window or you carry yesterday's fatigue into the Leg Press. Push the 6 toward an 8 and every main lift moves faster without changing a single set.

cards.title: Three Lifts — Leave The Fourth Alone
cards.body: You have 4 training days but the app is deliberately 3 full-body sessions, and that gap is intentional, not a hole to fill. The fourth day is for your runs and recovery, not a bonus lift — your sleep quality is sitting at 6/10, and that's what's actually gating how much you can absorb right now. You're newer to structured progression, so there are months of clean linear gains ahead IF you don't borrow against recovery to chase them early. The discipline this cycle isn't doing more — it's showing up for exactly the three sessions and stopping when the prescription says stop. Bank the consistency now; we earn the right to add volume in Forge.

cards.title: The App Number Is Your Floor
cards.body: You're 1–2 years into structured progression, which means clean linear gains are still on the table — so treat the single number on each lift as the floor you start from, never a ceiling. Drive LOAD first on the two big mains, Leg Press and Hip Thrust: when you hit the top of the range clean at RPE 8 with two in the tank and your back is quiet, add weight next session. Add REPS, not load, on everything else — Goblet Squat, Back Extension, the carries — until those top out too. Two levers, two jobs: load the mains, build reps on the rest. Log it honestly each week and I drive the next jump from your numbers.

cards.title: Protein Floor: 180g A Day
cards.body: No food data came in at intake, so we set a baseline and refine it from your check-ins — start here, don't guess. At 90 kg, anchor to a protein floor of 2.0 g/kg: that's 180 g a day, the non-negotiable that lets a newer-to-progression lifter actually build the lean muscle this hybrid arc is chasing. Split it across four feeds of ~40 g so the signal stays high all day rather than one big dinner hit. This is the one number I want locked before we touch calories — recomp lives or dies on protein, and we'll only adjust it if the weekly logs say so.

cards.title: Bank The Runs, Don't Race Them
cards.body: You're a fresh runner, so your two easy runs this week have one job: keep your heart rate under 140 and your breathing conversational, walk-run intervals included. The most common way new hybrid athletes blow up Cycle 1 is turning easy runs into hard ones because they feel fine in the moment — and that overcooks the recovery your lifts need. There's zero run intensity in your plan until Crossover, so going faster now buys you nothing and costs you a quiet back. Treat sub-140 as a hard ceiling, the same way RPE 8 is a hard ceiling in the gym. Showing up easy and consistent is the win, not the pace on the watch.

cards.title: Why No Deadlift Yet
cards.body: You know the deadlift builds backs — so let's be clear this isn't fear, it's sequencing. With a chronic back and only 1–2 years of structured progression, the loaded free hinge is the highest-skill, least-forgiving way to load your spine, and right now your brace hasn't earned the right to defend it under a moving bar. So Bedrock gives you the same hinge stimulus through the 45° Back Extension and Hip Thrust — trunk-supported, high-SFR, almost nothing lost — while we build the anti-extension and anti-rotation strength that becomes the deadlift's permission slip. Forge is where the free hinge returns. Five clean weeks of bracing buys you that bar.

cards.title: Capped At 8 Is The Fast Lane
cards.body: You've got 1–2 years of gym time but you're new to programmed progression — and that's exactly why leaving 2 reps in the tank wins. With clean linear progress still on the table, the limiter on your gains isn't how hard a single set feels, it's how many quality sessions you stack without a back flare or a recovery hole. RPE 8 on Leg Press and Hip Thrust means you finish every set repeatable, log it honestly, and add load next week — that compounding beats any heroic top set that costs you the next session. You're not holding back; you're refusing to trade three good weeks for one ego rep.
